message.content: We need to settle on an infrastructure-as-code tool for the platform team. The main contenders are Terraform, Pulumi, and AWS CDK. The team already has a fair amount of HCL experience and we maintain an internal module registry that everyone pulls from. I want to weigh those factors and come to a decision we can document for the wider org.

content.text: Let me check what the existing module registry expects before recommending.

message.content: Right, the registry is all HCL modules and most of the team can already read and write Terraform comfortably. Pulumi would let us use a real programming language, and CDK would tie us closely to AWS, but neither advantage outweighs throwing away the module registry and the existing HCL fluency. The retraining cost is the part I keep coming back to — it's not just learning syntax, it's rebuilding the shared module library and the review habits around it.

content.text: Agreed — the existing registry plus HCL fluency is the deciding factor. Terraform it is.

message.content: Let's lock it in: Terraform for infrastructure management, chosen on team HCL familiarity and the existing org module registry, with Pulumi and CDK both rejected. Please write this up as a short decision record so the rest of the platform org has something concrete to point at when they ask why we didn't go with a general-purpose language option. Make sure the writeup notes that the deciding factor was the existing HCL module registry and team fluency rather than any inherent weakness in Pulumi or CDK, because both are perfectly good tools — the cost here is migration and retraining, not capability. I'd also like a short note that we should revisit this if the module registry ever gets retired, so the decision doesn't ossify into dogma.
